title: Dog Licking Paws Until Raw: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog licking their paws raw? Learn about causes, why it is a medium-urgency issue, and how to triage this painful condition.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive paw licking is usually caused by allergies or infections and requires a vet visit within 24-48 hours, or sooner if pus or limping occurs. Providing a photo to your vet can assist in diagnosis and help track the healing process.

What causes my dog to lick their paws excessively?

When a dog licks their paws until the skin becomes raw, red, or bleeding, it is usually a sign of an underlying medical or behavioral issue. Clinically referred to as pododermatitis, this behavior is often triggered by environmental allergies (like grass or pollen), food sensitivities, or parasitic irritations such as mites or fleas. The constant moisture from saliva breaks down the skin barrier, often leading to secondary yeast or bacterial infections that make the itching even worse.

Is my dog's paw licking a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for this condition is Medium, typically requiring an appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Seek care sooner if the paws are oozing pus or have a foul odor.
  • Immediate attention is needed if your dog is limping or acting lethargic to prevent a localized irritation from turning into a systemic infection.

How does taking a photo of my dog's paws help with triage?

  • Clear photos allow the veterinary team to see the inflammation without causing the dog stress or pain during a physical exam.
  • Images help the vet identify "rusty" saliva staining and the exact location of the sores.
  • Photos are a vital tool for tracking the progression or healing of the skin lesions over time.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive paw licking in dogs, often unrecognized by owners as a sign of pruritus, can lead to self-trauma and secondary skin lesions such as alopecia, seborrhea, and bacterial or yeast pyoderma. A thorough dermatologic examination is crucial, documenting lesion type and distribution, alopecia, odor, scale, and skin/coat texture. Follicular papules, pustules, crusts, and epidermal collarettes usually indicate superficial pyoderma, while hyperpigmentation suggests chronic irritation (pruritus, infection, or inflammation). Concurrent bacterial and yeast infections should be excluded with a 21-30 day course of an antibiotic (e.g., cephalexin 30 mg/kg PO bid) and a systemic antifungal (e.g., ketoconazole, itraconazole, or fluconazole 5-10 mg/kg/day PO). If pruritus resolves, the initial cause was likely a microbial infection; if it persists or only improves, allergies are the most likely underlying cause (assuming parasitic causes have been excluded).

Chapter: Dermatology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 844)

faqs.question: Should I use an Elizabethan collar (cone)?
faqs.answer: Yes. A cone is the most effective way to break the lick-itch cycle and prevent your dog from causing further self-trauma until they can be examined by a professional.

faqs.question: Can I wash my dog's paws with human soap?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid harsh human soaps which can further disrupt the skin's pH. Use only a veterinarian-recommended antiseptic wash or plain, cool water to rinse off allergens.

faqs.question: Is paw licking always caused by allergies?
faqs.answer: While allergies are common, paw licking can also be a sign of anxiety, boredom, or a foreign body (like a thorn or foxtail) stuck between the toes.
